coredata.dc:title: A water-soluble fluorescent probe for ClO− and Cd2+ under physiological pH and its applications in living cells imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Pinyi
Zhang, Bo
Diao, Quanping
Li, Lin
Sun, Ying
Wang, Xinghua
Song, Daqian
coredata.dc:description: A water-soluble fluorescence probe (TF) derived from fluorescein has been developed. This probe can be used to detect ClO− and Cd2+ under physiological condition based on oxidation and coordination reactions, exhibiting almost 64-fold and 4-fold fluorescence enhancement. The experimentally observed changes in the structure and electronic properties of the probe in the presence of ClO− and Cd2+ were modeled by density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) computational calculations. Moreover, the present probe was successfully applied to the fluorescence imaging of ClO− and Cd2+ in living cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence probe
Fluorescein
Water-soluble
ClO−
Cd2+
Living cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of anions and determination of ClO−
Effect of metal ions and determination of Cd2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Special-funded Programme on National Key Scientific Instruments and Equipment Development (No. 2012YQ090194 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405057 , 21207047 and 21105037 ), and Science and Technology Developing Foundation of Jilin Province (Nos. 20150311099YY and 20150204017YY ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Normalized fluorescence intensities (I/I0) of TF (10μmolL−1) in HEPES aqueous buffer (50mmolL−1, pH=7.4) solution at 520nm, in the presence of different anions (a) and metal ions (b) (ClO−: 15μmolL−1; H2O2: 100μmolL−1; HO: 0.5mmolL−1 (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2 +100μmolL−1 H2O2; ONOO−: 0.5mmolL−1 NaNO2 +0.5mmolL−1 H2O2; Cd2+: 100μmolL−1; Other anions and metal ions: 200μmolL−1). Excitation wavelength was 495nm.

Fluorescence spectra of TF (10μmolL−1) with increasing amount of ClO− in HEPES aqueous buffer (50mmolL−1, pH=7.4). Inset: A linear calibration curve between the normalized fluorescence intensity at 520nm and the concentration (λex =495nm, slit: 2.5nm/2.5nm); Emission change under UV light (365nm) of TF and TF+ClO−.
Effect of different pH (50mmolL−1 Britton-Robinson buffer solution) at 520nm of TF and TF+ClO−. (λex =495nm, slit: 2.5nm/2.5nm).

Fluorescence spectra of TF (10μmolL−1) with increasing amount of Cd2+ in HEPES aqueous buffer (50mmolL−1, pH=7.4). Inset: A linear calibration curve between the normalized fluorescence intensity at 520nm and the concentration (λex =495nm, slit: 2.5nm/5.0nm); Emission change under UV light (365nm) of TF and TF+Cd2+.
Effect of different pH (50mmolL−1 Britton-Robinson buffer solution) at 520nm of TF and TF+Cd2+. (λex =495nm, slit: 2.5nm/5.0nm).
Possible mechanism of the response of TF towards ClO−.
Reaction mechanism with energy changes calculated at the DFT level.

Theoretical molding of oscillator strength (f) and relevant frontier molecular orbits of the main absorption at the TDDFT level based on the optimized structures of the ground state and the first excited state.

Fluorescence images of HeLa cells: (a) cells incubated with TF (10μmolL−1) for 30min; (b) cells incubated with NaClO (10μmolL−1) for another 10min; (c) cells incubated with Cd2+ (25μmolL−1) for another 30min.

head.ce:title: A water-soluble fluorescent probe for ClO− and Cd2+ under physiological pH and its applications in living cells imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Qianjin Street 2699, Changchun 130012, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Life Science, Anshan Normal University, Ping'an Street 43, Anshan 114005, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jilin Provincial Disability Rehabilitation Center, Guanggu Street 1555, Changchun 130015, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A water-soluble fluorescence probe (TF) derived from fluorescein has been developed. This probe can be used to detect ClO− and Cd2+ under physiological condition based on oxidation and coordination reactions, exhibiting almost 64-fold and 4-fold fluorescence enhancement. The experimentally observed changes in the structure and electronic properties of the probe in the presence of ClO− and Cd2+ were modeled by density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) computational calculations. Moreover, the present probe was successfully applied to the fluorescence imaging of ClO− and Cd2+ in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of simple analytical methods for the sensitive detection of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and toxic metals has attracted significant attention due to their effects on health of humans and animals [1–3]. Hypochlorite (ClO−), as a prominent member of ROS, is widely used, for example, NaClO is frequently used as a disinfectant and bleaching agent. ClO− plays a critical role in immune system against inflammation and microorganisms [4,5]. However, an excessive amount of ClO− could be harmful to the health of human beings, and can result in diseases such as arthritis, renal disease, lung injury, atherosclerosis, and cancers [6–10]. Among the toxic metal ions, cadmium ion (Cd2+) is an occupationally and environmentally toxic ion which is widely used in metallurgy, military industry and agriculture [11,12]. Cd2+ has a long elimination half-life (10–30 years) in the human body and is classified as a potent carcinogen [13]. Cd2+ can cause serious injury to the human lungs, kidneys, bone and nerve system, which results in bone disease, heart disease and diabetes [14,15]. In addition, ROS are also implicated in cadmium toxicology [16]. Therefore, a rapid and sensitive method for monitoring ClO− and Cd2+ in biological systems is highly important.

Fluorescent probes have been evaluated as powerful tools to detect chemical agents in recent years, due to their high sensitivity, high selectivity, easy operation and fast response, and were used not only in conventional experiments but also in vivo studies [17–23]. Especially for the living biological imaging research, the probes are advantageous because they cause less cell damage while displaying high spatial and time resolution capabilities in visualizing analytes [24,25]. Hence, the design and synthesis of novel fluorescent probes, which can recognize these anions and cations, are desirable and significant. Up to now, a number of fluorescent probes for ClO− [26–32] and Cd2+ [33–35] have been reported. However, those probes might encounter some problems, such as poor water solubility, weak selectivity, low quantum yield and a relatively short emission wavelength. Therefore, there is still need to further develop water-soluble, selective, and sensitive fluorescent probes for ClO− and Cd2+.

Fluorescein (C.I. Solvent Yellow 94) was widespread used in many areas, for example, as a fluorescent probe in biomarkers and detecting eye diseases [36,37]. It has high fluorescence quantum yield, high solubility in water, visible excitation and emission wavelength, low toxicity and strong interaction with biomolecules. Thus, in this work, we report a novel and water-soluble fluorescein-based fluorescent probe for detection of ClO− and Cd2+ (Fig. 1 ). This probe (TF) was derived from Schiff base fluorescein and exhibited highly selective and sensitive response to ClO− and Cd2+ in aqueous solution. The fluorescent changes are mainly due to the redox reaction of TF and ClO− and coordination reaction of TF and Cd2+, which were reasonably proved by combined experimental and computational study. Furthermore, the probe can also used to living cells imaging.

Fluorescein (95%) and 2-thiophenecarboxylic acid hydrazide (97%) were purchased from J&K Chemical Ltd. NaClO (5% available chlorine), KI, NaCl, KBr, NaF, CH3COOH, NaN3, NaN3, NaNO2, Na2S, H2O2, CdSO4·8/3H2O, LiCl, MgCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, MnSO4, FeCl2·4H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, Hg(NO3)2, CuCl2·5H2O, and AlCl3·6H2O were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Other reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification or treatment. All aqueous solutions were prepared with ultrapure water obtained by a Milli-Q water purification system (18.2MΩcm). A stock solution of 1mmolL−1 ClO− was quantified by iodometry. ClO− solutions at various concentrations were prepared by diluting the stock solution with ultrapure water. The living HeLa (human cervical adenocarcinoma) cells were provided by the Jilin University hospital of stomatology (China).

UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a TU-1810C UV–vis spectrometer (Beijing Purkinje General Inc., China) and a VARIAN Cary Eclipse spectrofluorophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA), respectively. Spectra of 1H NMR (TMS as internal standard) were measured on a Mercury 300BB nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (Varian Inc., USA). Mass spectra were measured on a LC/MS QTRAP spectrometer (AB SCIEX Inc., USA). FT-IR spectrum was recorded using KBr pellets on a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., USA) in the wavenumber region of 4000–400cm−1. All pH measurements were made with a PHS-3C pH-Meter (INESA Scientific Inc., China). Cells were imaged by an Olympus IX 51 inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus Corporation, Japan) equipped with integrated color filters.
The synthetic route of TF is shown in Fig. 1.

AF was synthesized from fluorescein according to the reported method [38]. 2.0g of fluorescein (6mmol) and 3.5mL of CH3OH were put into a 100mL bottom flask. Then 8.5mL of 50% NaOH solution, 2.0mL of CHCl3 (25mmol), and 30mg of dibenzo-18-crown-6 (catalytic amount) were added in the flask. The reaction temperature was maintained at 55°C and the mixture was stirred for 16h. Having been cooled, the mixture was acidified with 8molL−1 H2SO4. The precipitate was purified by chromatography on a silica gel column with CH2Cl2-ethyl acetate (10:1, v:v) as the elution solvent. Finally, 0.67g of AF as a light yellow solid (yield 31%) was obtained. ESI-HR-MS, m/z: 383.1468 [M+Na]+ (calcd. 383.0526 for C21H12NaO6 +). 1H NMR (300MHz, DMSO-d6), δ: 11.88(s, 1H), 10.63(s, 1H), 10.26(s, 1H), 8.02(d, 1H), 7.85-7.70(m, 2H), 7.30(s, 1H), 6.96(d, 1H), 6.85(s,1H), 6.71(d, 1H), 6.61(s, 2H).

0.18g of AF (0.5mmol) and 71mg of 2-thiophenecarboxylic acid hydrazide (0.5mmol) were mixed in 10mL of CH3OH, following by stirring at room temperature for 12h. The resulting precipitate was filtered and washed 3 times with 15mL CH3OH. Finally, 0.18g of TF as dark yellow solid (yield 75%) was obtained. ESI–MS, m/z: 485.0 [M+H]+. FT-IR (KBr, cm−1): 3453, 1753, 1633, 1354, 1282, 1221, 1165, 1119. 1H NMR (300MHz, DMSO-d6), δ: 12.63 (s, 1H), 12.54 (s, 1H), 10.31 (s, 1H), 9.20 (s, 1H), 8.11-7.90 (m, 3H), 7.78 (dtd, 2H), 7.38-7.24 (m, 2H), 6.80-6.56 (m, 5H).

The probe TF was dissolved in DMF to get a 10mmolL−1 stock solution. Before spectroscopic measurement, the solution was freshly prepared in HEPES aqueous buffer (50mmolL−1, pH=7.4) by diluting the high concentration stock solution to 10μmolL−1. ClO− and Cd2+ were detected by the addition of different concentration stock solutions of ClO− and Cd2+ to TF (10μmolL−1) solution. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in the wavelength range of 500–650nm with excitation wavelength at 495nm. Both the excitation and emission slits were set at 2.5nm/2.5nm for ClO− and 2.5nm/5.0nm for Cd2+, respectively.

The HeLa Cells were grown in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM) supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin–streptomycin, incubated under an humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 and 95% air at 37°C for 24h. Cells were seeded on dish for fluorescence microscopic imaging by inversion fluorescence microscope.

Spectroscopic measurements of TF are performed in aqueous medium at physiological pH (50mmolL−1 HEPES buffer, pH 7.4) under ambient conditions. TF displays major absorption band with the maximum absorbance peak at 498nm in HEPES buffer, which is characteristic of fluorescein under this condition. The free TF upon excitation at 495 nm gives only a weak fluorescence (Φ=0.11) due to highly efficient photoinduced electron transfer (PET) from the receptor to the fluorophore. An important feature of optical probe is its special selectivity for a kind of analyte over other substances. In order to study the recognition ability of TF for anions and metal ions, we carried out the experiments with fluorescence and UV–vis spectrometer. The results are shown in Fig. 2 , Figs. S6 and S7. However, as shown in Fig. S7, the selectivity of the UV–vis spectrometry is poor. Therefore, the present study focused on the fluorescence spectrometry.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of anions and determination of ClO−
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2a shows the effects of some anions on the normalized fluorescence intensities (I/I0), where I0 and I are fluorescence intensities of the probe in the absence and the presence of the anions, respectively. The probe (10μmolL−1) displayed significant enhancement of fluorescence intensity (λex =495nm, λem =520nm, slit width: 2.5nm/2.5nm) in the presence of the ClO− (∼64-fold for 2.5 equiv. of ClO− ion). Therefore, the TF can serve as a selective fluorescent probe for determining ClO− ions in the presence of other competing anions.

A fluorescence spectrum titration for ClO− was carried out. Fig. 3 showed the changes in the fluorescence spectrum when ClO− solution was added in 10μmolL−1 TF solution in ultrapure water (2mL). We could see that the ClO− induced obvious changes in fluorescence intensity under illumination with a 365nm UV lamp. The normalized fluorescence intensities (I/I0) at 520nm were linearly proportional to the concentrations of ClO− from 0 to 25μmolL−1, suggesting the potential application for quantitative determination of ClO−. The linear regression equation of calibration curve is I/I0 =2.53C-0.67 (C: μmolL−1) with the correlation coefficient of 0.9992. The intra-day precision obtained is lower than 5%. The limit of detection (LOD) for ClO− was estimated to be 0.068μmolL−1 based on 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of blank solution and k is the slope of calibration curve, which was lower than there obtained with previously reported probes (Table S1).

The effect of pH on the recognition behavior of TF toward ClO− was also studied. The results are shown in Fig. 4 . Fluorescence intensity of TF itself is not very sensitive to pH. However, fluorescence intensity of TF+ClO− changes significantly with the change of pH value and are highest in the range of 6–7. This result suggests that this probe can work under physiological pH conditions and therefore has potential for biological applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of metal ions and determination of Cd2+
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2b shows the effects of some metal ions on the normalized fluorescence intensities (I/I0), where I0 and I are fluorescence intensities of the probe in the absence and the presence of the metal ions, respectively. And they all triggered minor changes except Cd2+, Cu2+ and Al3+. The fluorescence intensity decreasing of Cu2+ and Al3+ could be ascribed to the paramagnetic quenching effect [39] and ligand to metal charge transfer (LMCT) mechanism [40]. The probe (10μmolL−1) displayed fluorescence intensity addition (λex =495nm, λem =520nm, slit width: 2.5nm/5.0nm) in the presence of the Cd2+ (∼4-fold for 10 equiv. of Cd2+ ion). Therefore, the TF can serve as a selective fluorescent probe for determining Cd2+ ions in the presence of other competing metal ions.

A fluorescence spectrum titration for Cd2+ was also carried out by the similar method to detecting ClO−. Fig. 5 shows the changes in the fluorescence spectrum when Cd2+ solution is added into TF (10μmolL−1) aqueous solution (2mL). The normalized fluorescence intensities (I/I0) at 520nm were linearly proportional to the concentrations of Cd2+ from 0 to 100μmolL−1. The linear regression equation of calibration curve is I/I0 =0.0297C–1.03 (C: μmolL−1) with the correlation coefficient of 0.9987. The intra-day precision obtained is lower than 5%. The LOD for Cd2+ was estimated to be 2.2 μmol L−1, which was lower than there obtained with previously reported probes (Table S2).

Fig. 6 shows the effect of pH on fluorescence intensities at 520nm. As expected, in the pH range from 6 to 7, TF shows a sensitive fluorescence enhancing response to Cd2+. Therefore, the fluorescent probe TF also could be used to monitor Cd2+ in biological systems.

To investigate the reaction mechanism, the ESI–MS spectra were used to study the oxidation reaction of TF and ClO− and coordination reaction of TF and Cd2+. The ESI–MS spectrum of TF revealed a main peak at m/z =485.0 corresponding to [TF+H]+. When 1.5 equiv. of ClO− was added into the solution, a peak corresponding to AF appeared at m/z =383.1 ([AF+Na]+) (Fig. S8). According to previous studies [41,42], the plausible mechanism of the interaction between TF and ClO− is presented in Fig. 7 .

ClO− is first attacked the imino group of TF, which results in the loss of the 2-thiophenecarboxylic acid hydrazide unit. This is facilitated by the nucleophilic electron donating effect of the lone pair on the N atom through extended conjugation that follows the nucleophilic attack by H2O with the elimination of HClO forming the AF molecule. When 5 equiv. of Cd2+ was added to the solution, a main peak at m/z =597.0 appeared, coinciding exactly with that of the positive ion [TF+Cd−H]+ (Fig. S8). Furthermore, Job’s plot analysis [43] (Fig. S9) based on fluorescence intensity was performed. The result showed the intersection point for fluorescence intensity appeared at the molar fraction of 0.50, suggesting that TF binds to Cd2+ in a ratio of 1:1.

To understand the relationship between the structural changes and the fluorescence enhancement of TF in the presence of ClO− and Cd2+, we have investigated the structural, electronic, and optical properties using density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent density functional theory (TD-DFT) calculations as implemented in the Gaussian 09 software [44]. The energy-optimized geometries of TF, AF and intermediate have been generated using B3LYP/6-31+G(d,p) basis sets, TF-Cd2+ using B3LYP/6-31+G(d,p)/LanL2DZ basis sets. For the TD-DFT calculation, solvent correction was incorporated by integral equation formalism polarizable continuum model (IEF-PCM) and water as the solvent. All the optimized geometries were confirmed to be local minimums due to the non-existence of imaginary frequency.

The possible interaction between TF and ClO− has been investigated using DFT calculation, and each of the probable intermediate state was optimized. The proposed intermediates are listed in Fig. 8 . A net lowering of energy of the molecule versus (722.34kJmol−1) was found. The theoretical calculation result was supported with prediction mechanism.

The TD-DFT calculation indicated that the S0 →S1 electronic transitions with oscillator strength f=0.6933, 0.5345 and 0.4777 are identified as the allowable transitions of TF, AF and TF-Cd2+, respectively. The electron distributions in HOMO, LUMO and LUMO+1 of TF, AF and TF-Cd2+ are shown in Fig. 9 . For probe TF, the HOMO is distributed primarily on xanthene and iminoyl (CN) unit, whereas the LUMO is transferred to thiazole and half fluorophore unit. The electrons distribution of HOMO and LUMO supported the PET process and this result suggested that TF was weak fluorescent. For probe AF, the molecular orbitals were localized on fluorophore for both HOMO and LUMO, so there was no electron transfer upon excitation and the AF showed a strong fluorescence. For probe TF-Cd2+, the two main absorptions were observed from S0 to S1, parts of electron (35.3%) would be transferred from the fluorophore unit to the thiazole unit, resulting in the PET occurs and part of fluorescence quenching. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of TF-Cd2+ is weaker than that of AF. From energy point of view, the energy gaps between the HOMO and LUMO in the TF and TF-Cd2+ were calculated to be 4.106eV and 3.175 eV, respectively, indicating that the binding of Cd2+ to TF decreases the HOMO–LUMO energy gap of the complex and stabilizes the system [45]. Thus, these results show a favorable complexation according to proposed coordination. In conclusion, based on the TD-DFT calculations, the optical properties and structural changes of TF, AF and TF-Cd2+ have been theoretically revealed.

Cell cytotoxicity assays were conducted using HeLa cells to test the cytotoxicity of TF. HeLa cells were passed and plated to ∼70% confluence in 96-well Plates 24h before treatment. Prior to TF treatment, DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% penicillin–streptomycin was removed and replaced with fresh DMEM, and 10mmolL−1 TF stock solutions were added to obtain final concentrations of 5, 10, 20, and 50μmolL−1 respectively. The treated cells were incubated for 24h at 37°C under 5% CO2 and 95% air. Subsequently, cells were treated with 5mgmL−1 MTT (40μLwell−1) and incubated for an additional 4h. Then the cells were dissolved in DMSO (150μLwell−1), and the absorbance at 570nm was recorded. The cell viability (%) was calculated according to the following equation:
Cell viability ( % )
=
OD sample
OD control
× 100
where ODsample represents the optical density of the wells treated with various concentration of TF and ODcontrol represents that of the wells treated. The percent of cell survival values is relative to untreated control cells. As shown in Fig. S10, the cell viability remains more than 80% after treated with 50μmolL−1 TF for 24h. The result indicated that TF was almost no cytotoxicity for long period incubation at low concentration and should be safe for bioimaging.

In order to examine the practical applicability of the probe in biological samples, fluorescence imaging experiments were conducted in living cells. As shown in Fig. 10 a, weak intracellular fluorescence of HeLa cells incubated with TF (10μmolL−1) for 30min was observed. Addition of 10μmolL−1 NaClO to the above-mentioned cells resulted in the remarkable enhancement of the intracellular fluorescence after 10min incubation (Fig. 10b). Addition of 25μmolL−1 Cd2+ to the another one above-mentioned cells resulted in the weak enhancement of the intracellular fluorescence after 30min incubation (Fig. 10c). These results indicate that probe TF is cell membrane permeable and able to response to ClO− and Cd2+ in living cells.

In summary, we have demonstrated a novel fluorescent probe capable of selective detection of ClO− and Cd2+ in aqueous buffer solution. The fluorescence of the probe can be enhanced by ClO− and the LOD of ClO− was 0.068μmolL−1. The fluorescence of the probe can be enhanced Cd2+ and the LOD of Cd2+ was 2.2μmolL−1. The different binding mode and reaction mechanism of TF with ClO− and Cd2+ were verified by DFT/TDDFT calculation using Gaussian 09. The potential of the probe for practical application was confirmed by employing it for fluorescence imaging in human cancer cells.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Special-funded Programme on National Key Scientific Instruments and Equipment Development (No. 2012YQ090194), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21405057, 21207047 and 21105037), and Science and Technology Developing Foundation of Jilin Province (Nos. 20150311099YY and 20150204017YY).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.04.142.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: H2O2, a necessary evil for cell signaling
Nox enzymes, ROS, and chronic disease: an example of antagonistic pleiotropy
Design principles of fluorescent molecular sensors for cation recognition
Hypochlorous acid: a natural adjuvant that facilitates antigen processing, cross-priming, and the induction of adaptive immunity
Bacterial responses to reactive chlorine species
Myeloperoxidase and chlorinated peptides in osteoarthritis: potential biomarkers of the disease
Hypochlorous acid alters bronchial epithelial cell membrane properties and prevention by extracellular glutathione
Evidence for rapid inter- and intramolecular chlorine transfer reactions of histamine and carnosine Chloramines: implications for the prevention of hypochlorous-acid-mediated damage
Inflammation and cancer: role of phagocyte-generated oxidants in carcinogenesis
A water-stable lanthanide-functionalized MOF as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescent probe for Cd2+
Bioavailability of cadmium and lead in a soil amended with phosphorus fertilizers
Current status of cadmium as an environmental health problem
Long-term dietary cadmium intake and postmenopausal endometrial cancer incidence: a population-based prospective cohort study
Role of oxidative stress in cadmium toxicity and carcinogenesis
Small molecule-based ratiometric fluorescence probes for cations, anions, and biomolecules
Design strategies for lab-on-a-molecule probes and orthogonal sensing
One-pot green synthesis of carbon dots by using Saccharum officinarum juice for fluorescent imaging of bacteria (Escherichia coli) and yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae) cells
Preparation of multicolor emitting carbon dots for HeLa cell imaging
Synthesis of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots from dried shrimps for cell imaging and boldine drug delivery system
One-step hydrothermal approach to fabricate carbon dots from apple juice for imaging of mycobacterium and fungal cells
Far-red to near infrared analyte-responsive fluorescent probes based on organic fluorophore platforms for fluorescence imaging
FRET-Based small-molecule fluorescent probes: rational design and bioimaging applications
Development of imidazoline-2-thiones based two-photon fluorescence probes for imaging hypochlorite generation in a Co-Culture system
A ratiometric fluorescent probe for rapid and sensitive visualization of hypochlorite in living cells
A two-photon fluorescent probe for detecting endogenous hypochlorite in living cells
A fluorescent probe for hypochlorite based on the modulation of the unique rotation of the N–N single bond in acetohydrazide
A novel visual and far-red fluorescent dual-channel probe for the rapid and sensitive detection of hypochlorite in aqueous solution and living cells
A mitochondria-targeted two-photon fluorescent probe for highly selective and rapid detection of hypochlorite and its bio-imaging in living cells
A highly selective fluorescent bioimaging probe for hypochlorite based on 1,8-naphthalimide derivative
Reactive fluorescent dye functionalized cotton fabric as a Magic Cloth for selective sensing and reversible separation of Cd2+ in water
Synthesis of a phenazine-based 1 2,3-triazole from naturally occurring naphthoquinone designed as a probe for Cd2+ ions
Simple and sensitive colorimetric sensing of Cd2+ ion using chitosan dithiocarbamate functionalized gold nanoparticles as a probe
The stimulation of fluorescein in external ophthalmic photography
Characterization of fluorescein transport in isolated proximal tubular cells of the rat: evidence for mitochondrial accumulation
A reversible fluorescence chemosensor for sequentially quantitative monitoring copper and sulfide in living cells
Coumarin-derived Cu2+-selective fluorescence sensor: synthesis, mechanisms, and applications in living cells
A triphenyl amine-based solvatofluorochromic dye for the selective and ratiometric sensing of OCl− in human blood cells
A highly specific fluorescent probe for hypochlorite based on fluorescein derivative and its endogenous imaging in living cells
Formation and stability of inorganic complexes in solution
A sensitive fluorescent probe for Cu2+ based on rhodamine B derivatives and its application to drinking water examination and living cells imaging

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Pinyi Ma is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. His interest is spectral analysis.
Bo Zhang is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. Her interest is spectral analysis.
Quanping Diao is currently a PhD student in College of Chemistry, Jilin University. He is an associate professor in Anshan Normal University. His interest is spectral analysis.
Lin Li is physician-in-charge in Jilin Provincial Disability Rehabilitation Center. Her interest is neurology.
Ying Sun gained her doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2009 and she is an associate professor in that school. Her research area is spectral analysis.

Xinghua Wang gained his doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2006 and he is an associate professor in that school. His research areas are spectral and chromatography analysis.
Daqian Song gained his doctor’s degree from College of Chemistry, Jilin University in 2003 and he is a professor in that school. His research areas are spectral and chromatography analysis.
